metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the `static_tokens` config schema: e.g., a TOML array-of-tables `[[mcp.auth.static_tokens]]` with fields `token_hash` (hex SHA-256 of the raw bearer token), `client_id` (string), and `scopes` (list of strings), or document an alternative format with an example.
findings.finding: The format/structure of the `static_tokens` source is never specified — the plan says only 'static-token source' in config, but doesn't define whether this is a TOML inline list of token records, a file path to a secrets file, per-token env vars, or a secret-store URI. An executor cannot implement `StaticBearerVerifier` loading or write the config AC without this decision.
findings.location: What section, bullet 1 (`static_tokens` source); AC 1 (config exists); AC 4 (valid static token → 200)
findings.suggested_fix:
Add to the What section: `static_tokens` is a TOML array-of-tables, each entry having `token_hash` (lowercase hex SHA-256 of the raw bearer value), `client_id` (string), and `scopes` (list of strings). The raw token is never written to config; callers pre-hash it or use the provided CLI helper (S7). Example:
```toml
[[mcp.auth.static_tokens]]
token_hash = "e3b0c44298fc1c149afb..."
client_id = "ci-runner"
scopes = ["read"]
```

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the full schema of each `static_tokens` entry (required/optional fields: `token`, `client_id`, `scopes`; their types; how the token value is supplied before hashing) so the verifier implementation and AC #5 test are unambiguous.
findings.finding: The `static_tokens` config field is named and its per-token attributes (`client_id`, `scopes`) are mentioned in the Security section, but the schema of each token entry is never defined: the plan does not specify whether this is a TOML array-of-tables (`[[mcp.auth.static_tokens]]`), an inline list, what fields are required vs. optional, or how the plaintext token value is supplied (env reference, inline string, file path) before being hashed at rest. The `StaticBearerVerifier` implementation depends on this schema, as does the test that verifies no plaintext is retained.
findings.location: What section — `static_tokens source`; Security section — 'only a SHA-256 hash of each static token is held in memory'; AC #5
findings.suggested_fix: Add a schema block to the What section, e.g.: each entry is a TOML inline-table `{token = "<plaintext-or-env-ref>", client_id = "<string>", scopes = ["<scope1>", ...]}`. Specify that `token` is consumed once at startup (hashed, plaintext discarded), `client_id` and `scopes` are required.

findings.finding: The `static_tokens` source field is named but the schema of each individual token entry is never defined. The What section mentions 'per-token `client_id`/`scopes`' only in passing; the plan never specifies the full field list (plaintext token value, client_id, scopes, optional audience override, etc.) or types that the `StaticBearerVerifier` must deserialize from config.
findings.location: What section — 'the static-verifier params (`static_tokens` source)'; 'per-token `client_id`/`scopes`'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Static token entry schema' subsection under What, e.g.: each entry is a TOML inline-table `{token = "…", client_id = "…", scopes = ["…"]}`. The verifier hashes `token` on load and discards the plaintext; `client_id` and `scopes` populate the returned `AccessToken`; audience defaults to the global `resource_server_url` unless overridden per-entry.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (with a unit or integration test) that verifies the server refuses to start (raises a clear config error) when `mcp.auth.enabled=true` but the auth config is invalid or self-contradictory (e.g., empty `strategies`, missing required fields) — observable oracle: startup raises a named exception or the test asserts the server is not reachable.
findings.finding: The Security section states 'fail-closed (verifier error or misconfigured auth → deny / refuse start)' but no Acceptance Criterion or test covers the 'refuse start' / startup-validation failure path — e.g., `mcp.auth.enabled=true` with an empty or internally inconsistent strategy list, or a static-token entry missing required fields. This is a testable security-critical failure path with no observable oracle in the ACs.
findings.location: Security section; Acceptance Criteria (no AC present for startup misconfiguration)
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC: 'Unit/integration test asserts that constructing `CompositeTokenVerifier` with an empty verifier list, or `build_server` with `mcp.auth.enabled=true` and no valid strategy, raises a `ConfigurationError` (or equivalent) at startup — not a silent denial at request time.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how the flat `McpConfig` dataclass and `_SECTIONS['mcp']` dispatch table are extended to accommodate the nested `[mcp.auth]` sub-group (e.g., a new top-level `[auth]` section, or a new nested-section coercion mechanism in `coerce_sparse`/`_SECTION_CLASSES`).
findings.finding: The plan asserts that `[mcp.auth]` config can be added as a nested sub-group inside `McpConfig` following the 'existing coercer pattern', but the codebase shows `McpConfig` is a flat 3-field dataclass (`readonly`, `allow_llm`, `allow_jira_sync`) and `coerce_sparse` processes each top-level section as a flat key→value dict dispatched via `_SECTIONS['mcp']` — there is no nested sub-table mechanism in the current infrastructure. Adding `[mcp.auth]` as a TOML sub-group would silently be ignored (unknown key warning) or raise a ConfigError without structural changes to coerce_sparse, _SECTION_CLASSES, and McpConfig.
findings.location: What section — '[mcp.auth] group' / src/rebar/_config_schema.py:375-378 and 513-517
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) introduce auth fields as flat `mcp.*` keys (e.g., `mcp.auth_enabled`, `mcp.auth_strategies`) mirroring the existing pattern — requiring only field additions to McpConfig and coercer entries in _SECTIONS['mcp'] — or (b) add a new top-level `[auth]` section with its own `AuthConfig` dataclass and `_SECTIONS['auth']` entry, and update `_SECTION_CLASSES` and `Config` accordingly. Option (a) is the path of least resistance given the existing infra.

findings.finding: The plan asserts `[mcp.auth]` can be added as a nested sub-group inside `McpConfig` following 'the existing coercer pattern', but the existing `McpConfig` is a flat 3-field dataclass and `_SECTIONS['mcp']` is a flat key→coercer dict. The `coerce_sparse` function iterates `_SECTIONS[sect]` for scalar coercers only; no mechanism exists for a nested sub-table within a section. Other nested configs (e.g., `llm`) use the `_RESERVED_SECTIONS` escape hatch rather than the coercer pipeline. The plan never acknowledges this structural gap.
findings.location: What section — 'Extend `McpConfig` with an `[mcp.auth]` group'; AC1
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) flatten the auth fields into `McpConfig` (e.g., `auth_enabled`, `auth_strategies`, …) and register them in `_SECTIONS['mcp']` as scalar coercers, matching the existing pattern exactly; or (b) add `'mcp_auth'` as a new top-level `_SECTION_CLASSES`/`_SECTIONS` entry with its own coercer dict, and use TOML `[tool.rebar.mcp_auth]`. Either path requires updating the plan's AC1 to name the exact coercer registration.

findings.finding: The plan names `[mcp.auth]` as a TOML sub-table (a nested group under `[mcp]`) but the config infrastructure — `McpConfig` (a flat dataclass), `_SECTIONS['mcp']` (flat key→coercer dict), and `coerce_sparse` (processes only top-level sections) — has no mechanism to parse nested sub-sections. The plan never enumerates `_SECTIONS['mcp']` and `_SECTION_CLASSES` in `_config_schema.py` as edit targets, nor does it specify how the nested `[mcp.auth]` TOML sub-table will be parsed (new top-level section, nested dataclass + sub-section coercer, or flat keys under `[mcp]`).
findings.location: plan section 'What': 'Extend `McpConfig` with an `[mcp.auth]` group'; `src/rebar/_config_schema.py:375-378`, `478-517`, `462-475`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Before finalizing the integration-test ACs, confirm the exact method/attribute name that exposes a Streamable-HTTP ASGI app from FastMCP at mcp>=1.28 (e.g., by reading the mcp 1.28 release notes or the SDK's simple-auth example) and name it explicitly in the plan rather than deferring with 'resolved at implementation time'.
findings.finding: `streamable_http_app()` — the ASGI entrypoint the integration tests are written against — is UNRESOLVED in the installed mcp environment. The plan relies on this method for the `httpx ASGITransport` integration tests (AC lines 2–4) but names it only as 'the concrete mcp SDK entrypoint resolved against the pinned mcp>=1.28,<2 at implementation time' without verifying the method name or calling convention exists.
findings.location: AC line 2: 'httpx ASGITransport against the server's Streamable-HTTP ASGI app (the concrete mcp SDK entrypoint resolved against the pinned mcp>=1.28,<2 at implementation time)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify structured log fields (verifier class, exception type, sanitized client hint) and at least one observable counter (e.g., `auth.verifier.error`, `auth.denied`) for verifier exceptions and denial events so operators can alert on auth-subsystem failures distinct from normal 401s.
findings.finding: The plan's only instrumentation spec for verifier failures is 'the composite logs it' — no structured log fields (e.g., verifier class, exception type, client_id), no metric counter for auth denials/verifier errors, and no alert specification. Operators cannot distinguish a crashed verifier from a normal denial, and cannot set up alerting on auth-subsystem failures.
findings.location: src/rebar/_mcp_auth.py description; Security section ('the composite logs it and immediately fails closed')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the _mcp_auth.py spec: on verifier exception, emit a WARN-level structured log with keys {verifier_class, exc_type, request_id} and increment a counter `mcp_auth_verifier_error_total{verifier=...}`; on any denial (no-accept or raise), increment `mcp_auth_denied_total{reason=...}`. Note these in the Acceptance Criteria as observable oracles.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the required injection path for raw static token values in the Security section — specify whether tokens may be supplied as TOML config values (plaintext on disk), must be injected via environment variable (preferred), or must come from a secrets manager — so the secret-lifecycle posture on the boundary-crossing path is explicit rather than ambiguous.
findings.finding: The plan states static tokens are 'loaded from config/secret-store' and that 'only a SHA-256 hash is held in memory (no plaintext retained)', but does not declare whether raw token values may appear in plaintext in a TOML config file on disk. The phrase 'config' in 'config/secret-store' implies TOML is a valid source, which would mean plaintext token material at rest on disk — undeclared and unmitigated for the boundary-crossing path.
findings.location: Security section ('Static tokens are loaded from config/secret-store'); What section ('static_tokens source')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Security section or AC1: 'Raw static token values MUST be supplied via environment variable (e.g., `REBAR_MCP_AUTH_STATIC_TOKENS`) or a secrets manager — not as plaintext values in a committed TOML config file. The config schema documents this constraint.' This is consistent with the existing pattern for `WEBHOOK_TOKEN` in `review_bot/config.py`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC1/AC7) that explicitly covers the config→verifier factory: at minimum, an unknown/misspelled strategy name must produce a clear startup error (fail-closed), and an empty `strategies` list with `enabled=true` must also fail closed.
findings.finding: The 'What' section names a 'config→verifier factory' as a distinct, named deliverable, but no acceptance criterion explicitly covers it — it is only exercised implicitly via the integration tests (AC2–AC4), which do not distinguish a factory failure from a verifier failure.
findings.location: What section ('a config→verifier factory') vs Acceptance Criteria (AC1–AC8)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Acceptance Criteria: 'Unit/integration test asserts that an unknown strategy name in `mcp.auth.strategies` and an `enabled=true` config with an empty `strategies` list each cause a startup-time error (server refuses to start), not a silent pass-through.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify (by reading the installed FastMCP `__init__` signature) that `token_verifier` and `auth` are valid `FastMCP` constructor kwargs in the target mcp version, and explicitly add bumping `mcp>=1.28` (or whichever version introduces these kwargs) in `pyproject.toml` as a named deliverable in the What/AC sections.
findings.finding: The plan's core wiring call — `pass token_verifier=CompositeTokenVerifier(…) and auth=AuthSettings(…) to FastMCP` — asserts that `FastMCP` accepts `token_verifier` and `auth` as constructor keyword arguments. `FastMCP` is confirmed importable, but `token_verifier` as a `FastMCP` constructor kwarg is UNRESOLVED in the installed environment, and the installed `mcp` lower bound is `mcp>=1.9` while the plan's integration test hedges 'resolved against the pinned `mcp>=1.28,<2` at implementation time' — but the plan never specifies bumping the lower bound as a deliverable.
findings.location: What section — 'Wire it in build_server' / AC2 integration test description / pyproject.toml:82

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py` to the edit-target list and specify that line 208's hardcoded tuple must be replaced with `for var in (v['name'] for v in MCP_ENV_VARS if not v.get('deprecated')):` (or similar) so new auth env vars auto-appear in the Gate table.
findings.finding: `scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py` is an unenumerated consumer that hardcodes exactly the three existing gate env-var names at line 208 (`for var in ('REBAR_MCP_READONLY', 'REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_LLM', 'REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_JIRA_SYNC')`) rather than iterating `MCP_ENV_VARS`. New `REBAR_MCP_AUTH_*` vars added to `MCP_ENV_VARS` will NOT appear in the 'Gate environment variables' table in `docs/mcp-reference.md`, causing a CI drift-gate failure on the reference-doc check (`python scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py --check`) after regeneration, or silently omitting the new vars if not regenerated.
findings.location: What section ('update `MCP_ENV_VARS`/`server.json`'); AC1; `scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py` line 208
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section, add: 'Also update `scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py` line 208 to iterate `MCP_ENV_VARS` instead of the hardcoded 3-var tuple, so auth env vars appear in the generated `docs/mcp-reference.md` Gate table.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add 'bump `pyproject.toml` mcp lower bound to `>=1.28,<2`' as an explicit story deliverable, and add an AC or note confirming `FastMCP(token_verifier=…, auth=AuthSettings(…))` constructor kwargs and `streamable_http_app()` (or its correct name) exist at that version.
findings.finding: The plan's two load-bearing SDK capability assertions — `FastMCP(token_verifier=CompositeTokenVerifier(…), auth=AuthSettings(…))` constructor kwargs and `streamable_http_app()` as the ASGI integration-test entrypoint — are explicitly qualified as 'resolved against the pinned `mcp>=1.28,<2` at implementation time', but the current `pyproject.toml` lower bound is `mcp>=1.9` and bumping it to `>=1.28` is not listed as a deliverable of this story. Neither `token_verifier` as a `FastMCP` kwarg nor `streamable_http_app()` resolves in the currently installed (`mcp>=1.9`) environment, leaving both assertions unverified against the pinned floor.
findings.location: What section ('pass `token_verifier=CompositeTokenVerifier(…)` and `auth=AuthSettings(…)` to `FastMCP`'); AC2 ('the server's Streamable-HTTP ASGI app (the concrete `mcp` SDK entrypoint resolved against the pinned `mcp>=1.28,<2` at implementation time)'); pyproject.toml lines 82 and 149
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section's deliverable list, add: 'Bump `pyproject.toml` (and `dev` extra) `mcp` lower bound from `>=1.9` to `>=1.28,<2`; verify `FastMCP.__init__` accepts `token_verifier` and `auth` kwargs and that the Streamable-HTTP ASGI entrypoint method name at that version.' Add a matching AC: 'pyproject.toml declares `mcp>=1.28,<2` for both the `mcp` and `dev` extras.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly name the `mcp` version-bound bump to `mcp>=1.28,<2` as a required What deliverable and confirm (via a quick script or reading the mcp 1.28 changelog/source) that `FastMCP(token_verifier=…, auth=AuthSettings(…))` is a valid constructor signature at that version before committing the full plan.
findings.finding: The plan's central wiring calls — `FastMCP(token_verifier=CompositeTokenVerifier(…), auth=AuthSettings(…))` — assert specific FastMCP constructor kwargs that are UNVERIFIED against the installed mcp environment. The installed bound is mcp>=1.9 (pyproject.toml line 82, line 149), but the plan qualifies the integration test AC with '(the concrete mcp SDK entrypoint resolved against the pinned mcp>=1.28,<2 at implementation time)'. If `token_verifier=` and `auth=` were introduced only at mcp>=1.28, the plan cannot be implemented against the current lower bound and the version-bump is a required deliverable that is only mentioned parenthetically in an AC rather than as an explicit What/scope task.
findings.location: What section ('Wire it in build_server'); AC line 2 ('httpx ASGITransport against the server's Streamable-HTTP ASGI app (the concrete mcp SDK entrypoint resolved against the pinned mcp>=1.28,<2 at implementation time)')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a bullet under What: 'Bump pyproject.toml mcp lower bound to mcp>=1.28,<2 and verify FastMCP.__init__ accepts token_verifier and auth kwargs at that version.' Then remove the parenthetical from the AC and make it an explicit task.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the canonical `strategies` identifier for the static verifier (e.g., `"static"`) so the factory mapping and future stories (S3=`"jwks"`, S4=`"introspection"`) follow a consistent convention.
findings.finding: The valid string values for `strategies` in `[mcp.auth]` are not enumerated. The plan describes the field as 'an ordered list selecting which verifiers are active' but never states what identifiers an executor should use (e.g., `'static'`, `'jwks'`, `'introspection'`). An executor writing the factory and config validation cannot define the allowed enum without guessing.
findings.location: What section, bullet 1 (`strategies` field description)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a parenthetical to the `strategies` description: 'allowed values for S2: `["static"]`; future stories add `"jwks"` (S3), `"introspection"` (S4), `"proxy"` (S5), `"custom"` (S6).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace `/.well-known/oauth-protected-resource/…` in AC 3 with the actual path the `mcp>=1.28,<2` SDK serves (e.g. `/.well-known/oauth-protected-resource` or `/.well-known/oauth-protected-resource/<resource_server_url_encoded>`), or explicitly instruct the executor to derive the path from the `resource_metadata` URL returned in the `WWW-Authenticate` header of AC 2.
findings.finding: The PRM endpoint path in AC 3 contains a literal ellipsis (`…`) placeholder: `/.well-known/oauth-protected-resource/…`. An executor writing the integration test assertion cannot anchor the URL without knowing the actual path suffix, creating a fixture-invalidity risk (defect taxonomy item 4).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC 3: 'Integration test asserts the PRM document is served at `/.well-known/oauth-protected-resource/…`'
findings.suggested_fix: Rewrite AC 3 to: 'Integration test derives the PRM URL from the `resource_metadata` parameter in the `WWW-Authenticate` header returned by AC 2's 401 response, then GETs that URL and asserts HTTP 200 + JSON body contains an `authorization_servers` field.' This makes the AC self-grounding against the SDK's actual routing.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the audience comparison rule explicitly: e.g., 'the `aud` claim (string or array) must contain `resource_server_url` as an element (exact string match after URL normalization per RFC 3986)'.
findings.finding: The audience validation rule is stated as 'a token whose audience is not this resource server is rejected' but the comparison logic is not defined — it is unclear whether the `aud` claim must exactly equal `resource_server_url`, must contain it as a list member, or must match some normalized form. This is implementation-load-bearing for the `StaticBearerVerifier` and the AC that tests wrong-audience rejection.
findings.location: What section ('Mandatory audience validation'); AC 5 ('wrong-audience token is rejected')
findings.suggested_fix:
Add to the 'Mandatory audience validation' paragraph: 'The base verifier checks that the token's `aud` claim — whether a JSON string or array — contains `resource_server_url` as an exact member (case-insensitive scheme+host+path, trailing slash stripped). Tokens with an absent or non-matching `aud` are rejected (return None).'

For the static verifier this means the per-token record must carry an expected audience value (defaulting to `resource_server_url`) that is checked against a field the caller supplies.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or CLAUDE.md section capturing the composite-verifier contract (fail-closed on raise, fall-through on None, mandatory audience validation per RFC 8707) so S3–S6 implementors inherit the invariants without re-reading this story.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel composite-verifier auth framework with non-obvious contracts (fail-closed on exception, mandatory audience validation, ordered strategy selection) but does not include any ADR, CLAUDE.md entry, or design-doc update to capture these architectural decisions.
findings.location: ## Approach & rationale / ## Security / Acceptance Criteria
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `docs/adr/0NNN-auth-composite-verifier.md` (or a `## Auth framework` section in CLAUDE.md) that records: (1) the `TokenVerifier` protocol contract and ordering semantics, (2) the fail-closed error policy, (3) the always-on audience validation rationale, and (4) the extension point for future verifiers. Reference it from the plan's 'Approach & rationale' section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or prose spec) that the config factory validates each `strategies` entry against known verifier names and each static-token entry against required fields, emitting a human-readable `ConfigurationError` naming the offending key/value on startup failure.
findings.finding: The plan covers operator-facing misconfiguration ('refuse start' for misconfigured auth) at a coarse level but does not specify the error-surfacing UX for specific invalid config inputs — e.g., an unrecognized strategy name in `strategies`, a malformed static_token entry (missing hash field, wrong hash length), or an unparseable `issuer_url`. The operator has no specified diagnostic output to act on.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria item 1; Security section ('misconfigured auth → deny / refuse start')
findings.suggested_fix: In the factory spec, enumerate the valid strategy identifiers and required fields per static-token entry; specify that unrecognized/malformed entries raise a `McpConfigError` with a message like: 'Unknown auth strategy "statik_bearer"; valid values: ["static_bearer"]'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `CompositeTokenVerifier` + `strategies` config key are needed at S2, or whether `StaticBearerVerifier` can implement `TokenVerifier` directly and the composite/factory deferred to S3 when a second verifier exists (Rule-of-Three: cite the second call-site that justifies the composite at S2, or defer it).
findings.finding: The `strategies` ordered-list config key and the full `CompositeTokenVerifier` factory+dispatch infrastructure are introduced in S2 when only ONE verifier (static) exists at delivery time. The first-accept-wins ordering logic, strategy-name registry, and composite dispatch layer have no observable effect with a single strategy — the abstraction does not satisfy Rule-of-Three and is premature for this story.
findings.location: What section — 'strategies (ordered list selecting which verifiers are active — a composite, first-accept-wins)'; Scope section — 'JWKS/JWT (S3), introspection (S4), trusted-proxy (S5), custom (S6) are separate stories'
findings.suggested_fix: For S2: implement `StaticBearerVerifier` as the direct `TokenVerifier` passed to `FastMCP(token_verifier=StaticBearerVerifier(...))`. Add the `CompositeTokenVerifier` + `strategies` config key in S3 when JWKS/JWT becomes the second verifier, satisfying Rule-of-Three. The `[mcp.auth]` config group can still be introduced now (with `enabled`, `issuer_url`, `resource_server_url`, `required_scopes`, static-token params) — just omit `strategies` until S3.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name `_SECTIONS['mcp']` (in `_config_schema.py`) and `_SECTION_CLASSES` as mandatory parallel edit targets alongside `McpConfig`, with explicit coercer entries for each new auth field (`enabled`, `strategies`, `issuer_url`, `resource_server_url`, `required_scopes`, static-token source).
findings.finding: `_SECTIONS['mcp']` (at `_config_schema.py` lines 513-517) and `_SECTION_CLASSES` are mandatory parallel edit targets whenever `McpConfig` fields change, but the plan names neither as an explicit edit target. The `env_overrides()` function in `config.py` builds `REBAR_<SECT>_<KEY>` env-var names by iterating `_SECTIONS` — so new auth fields will only gain auto-derived env-var support if they are registered there. Without this edit, the `REBAR_MCP_AUTH_*` env vars named in AC1 will not be honoured.
findings.location: What section ('Auto-derive `REBAR_MCP_*` env vars'); AC1 (`REBAR_MCP_*` env vars); `src/rebar/_config_schema.py` lines 513-517 and 462-475
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What/Scope section: 'Also update `_SECTIONS["mcp"]` in `_config_schema.py` with a coercer entry for each new auth field, mirroring the existing `readonly`/`allow_llm`/`allow_jira_sync` entries, so `env_overrides()` auto-derives `REBAR_MCP_AUTH_*` env vars correctly.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile leaf AC7 with parent AC5: either (a) add REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_UNAUTHENTICATED_HTTP to the [mcp.auth] config group in S2's What section, add a build_server startup-refusal AC, and update AC7 to reflect the fail-fast behavior when HTTP is requested without auth and without the acknowledgment env var; or (b) explicitly assign this guard to another child story and note that assignment in S2's Scope.
findings.finding: S2's AC7 ('With mcp.auth.enabled=false the server behaves exactly as S1 — auth middleware absent') directly contradicts the parent epic's AC5, which requires that starting an HTTP transport with auth disabled FAILS FAST unless REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_UNAUTHENTICATED_HTTP is explicitly set. The parent also mandates this env var in its Security section ('no accidental open endpoint'). The leaf omits REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_UNAUTHENTICATED_HTTP from its [mcp.auth] config group additions entirely, meaning the parent's required acknowledgment mechanism has no home in S2. Since S2 is the story that wires auth into build_server, it is the natural owner of this guard.
findings.location: Leaf AC7 / What section ([mcp.auth] config group) vs. Parent epic AC5 and Security section
findings.suggested_fix: In S2's What section, add REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_UNAUTHENTICATED_HTTP to the enumerated REBAR_MCP_* env vars. Replace AC7 with two ACs: (1) 'With mcp.auth.enabled=false AND REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_UNAUTHENTICATED_HTTP unset, starting an HTTP transport raises a startup error (test asserts the refusal).' and (2) 'With mcp.auth.enabled=false AND REBAR_MCP_ALLOW_UNAUTHENTICATED_HTTP set, the server starts and behaves exactly as S1.' This aligns the leaf with the parent's fail-fast/no-accidental-open-endpoint requirement.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the TLS/transport-encryption posture for the authenticated HTTP endpoint in the Security section — either mandate HTTPS in production deployments (with a note that the `ASGITransport` test is exempt), or explicitly state 'TLS termination is operator responsibility and is a prerequisite for deploying with `mcp.auth.enabled=true`', so the security contract on the boundary-crossing path is complete.
findings.finding: The plan introduces bearer-token authentication on an HTTP endpoint (boundary-crossing, reachable by lower-trust actors) but never declares a TLS/transport-encryption posture. If deployed over plain HTTP, the Authorization header containing the bearer token transits in cleartext and is trivially interceptable. The Security section addresses constant-time compare, hash-at-rest, and no-logging but is entirely silent on encryption in transit.
findings.location: Security section; Acceptance Criteria (AC2/AC3/AC4); Compatibility section
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Security section: 'The authenticated HTTP endpoint MUST be placed behind TLS termination (HTTPS) in any deployment where `mcp.auth.enabled=true`; plain-HTTP deployment with auth enabled is unsupported and must be documented as such. The ASGI integration-test transport is exempt (in-process, no network path).' Optionally add a startup warning if `mcp.auth.enabled=true` and the server is not behind a detected TLS proxy.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the canonical string vocabulary for `strategies` values in the plan (e.g. `"static"` for S2, reserved names for S3–S6) so the config coercer, factory dispatch table, and future story authors share a single reference.
findings.finding: The `strategies` config key is described as 'an ordered list selecting which verifiers are active' but its valid value vocabulary — the string identifiers an operator may place in the list — is never defined anywhere in the plan. An implementer cannot know what string to register for the static verifier (e.g. `"static"`, `"static_bearer"`, `"pat"`) or what to validate/reject as unknown, and future stories (S3–S6) will need to add their own tokens to this same enum without a canonical reference.
findings.location: What section — `strategies` config key; also AC #1
findings.suggested_fix: Add a table to the What section enumerating valid `strategies` entries: `"static"` (this story), `"jwks"` (S3), `"introspection"` (S4), `"trusted_proxy"` (S5), `"custom"` (S6). State that the config coercer rejects unknown values at load time (fail-closed).

findings.finding: The Security section states 'fail-closed (verifier error … → deny)' but the What section describes the CompositeTokenVerifier as treating 'any error → None' (silently falling through to the next verifier). These are contradictory: if a verifier throws an exception, does the composite hard-deny the request (fail-closed) or skip that verifier and try the next (error→None)?
findings.location: Security section ('fail-closed … verifier error … → deny') vs. What section ('CompositeTokenVerifier … any error → None')
findings.suggested_fix: Adopt fail-closed at the composite level: if any active verifier throws an unexpected exception, the composite immediately returns a hard deny (raises / returns a sentinel that triggers 401) rather than falling through. Reserve 'error → None' only for expected negative results (token not recognized by this verifier), and distinguish that from exception/error. Update the What section to say 'expected rejection → None; unexpected exception → deny (fail-closed)'.

findings.finding: The CompositeTokenVerifier's error-handling policy is contradictory between the What and Security sections: What says 'any error → None' (silently fall through, try next verifier), but Security says 'fail-closed (verifier error … → deny)'. These are mutually exclusive behaviors on a security boundary: 'error → None' lets the composite continue to the next verifier (or resolve to 401), meaning an erroring verifier never hard-denies the request — a second active verifier could still accept it. Only when a single verifier is configured does None happen to produce a 401. With multiple strategies active this is a security-material mechanism gap.
findings.location: What section ('CompositeTokenVerifier … any error → None') vs. Security section ('fail-closed (verifier error or misconfigured auth → deny)')
findings.suggested_fix: Distinguish two cases: (a) a verifier returns `None` = 'I don't recognize this token' → try next; (b) a verifier raises an exception = internal error → log + treat as deny (raise, don't fall through). This preserves the composite's multi-strategy intent while honoring fail-closed on errors.

findings.finding: The CompositeTokenVerifier error-handling decision rule is contradictorily specified across two sections: the What section says 'any error → None' (silent fallthrough to the next verifier), while the Security section says 'fail-closed (verifier error … → deny)'. An implementor reading both sections cannot determine the intended behavior.
findings.location: What section — 'CompositeTokenVerifier (tries each configured verifier, first non-None AccessToken wins, any error → None)'; Security section — 'fail-closed (verifier error or misconfigured auth → deny / refuse start)'

findings.finding: AC2's observable oracle for the `WWW-Authenticate` header is stated as 'status + header value' but 'header value' is not anchored — the test could satisfy the AC by merely asserting `'www-authenticate' in response.headers` without validating that the header carries the RFC 9728 `resource_metadata` URL, which is the safety-critical property (clients must discover the PRM endpoint from this header).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2: 'observable oracle: status + header value'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'observable oracle: status + header value' with 'observable oracle: HTTP 401, `WWW-Authenticate` header value matches `re.search(r"resource_metadata=https?://", header_value)` (RFC 9728 §5.1)'.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
